prompt: For body composition, is HIIT better than moderate steady-state cardio? And what interval setups do you recommend?
expected_output: Eric summarized a meta-analysis (Steele et al.) finding no meaningful body-composition advantage of interval vs steady-state cardio: both produced small average changes (about -0.25 kg fat and +0.1 kg fat-free mass). He noted cardio is great for health and can help, but as a standalone fat-loss tool it is usually modest, so pairing with diet is more effective. For intervals, he gave concrete options: 30 s all-out (RPE 10) + 90 s easy recovery for 10-15 rounds; or 1 min hard / 1 min easy around RPE 9-10; or 2 min hard / 2 min easy at slightly lower intensity (about RPE 8-9.5) for fewer rounds. He also said interference with lifting is usually small if total stress is managed and sessions are programmed sensibly. Practical takeaway: choose the modality/protocol you can perform safely and adhere to.
topic: HIIT vs steady-state for fat loss
assertions: Answer captures the core SBS position on HIIT vs steady-state for fat loss
